entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845) drops off the record after the 1860 census — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 federal censuses have not been searched at all. These three enumerations together fill the 48-year gap noted in the project timeline. The 1880 census is especially valuable: it introduced the relationship-to-head column, so if Thomas Flynn is living with Patrick (or any sibling) in 1880, his relationship would be explicitly stated. All three years are fully indexed and freely searchable on FamilySearch. Search Patrick Flynn first; note all household members in each enumeration. Also review neighboring households for known Flynn associates — FAN cluster neighbors in a tight-knit Irish coal-mining community frequently emigrated from the same Irish townland.

Search Thomas Flynn (b. ~1818, Ireland) in the 1870 and 1880 censuses — the candidate father's own adult records are a core FAN item. In 1870 Thomas would be ~52; in 1880 he would be ~62. If he is still alive and heading a household in 1880, the relationship column would explicitly list every child remaining with him as 'son' or 'daughter,' directly identifying siblings and corroborating the parent-child pattern. Any siblings identified here become targets for death-certificate and baptismal-record searches in subsequent steps. Also note Thomas's wife's name — still unknown from current evidence — which would be required to complete the parentage conclusion.

Search for Patrick Flynn's marriage record in Pennsylvania Church and Town Records 1708-1985 (FamilySearch collection 1401638) and Pennsylvania County Marriages 1885-1950 (collection 1921317). Patrick was almost certainly Catholic — Irish immigrants in the Schuylkill coal towns (Pottsville, Minersville, Tamaqua) overwhelmingly attended Catholic parishes. Catholic marriage registers routinely record both parties' parents by name, including the groom's father. A Catholic marriage entry for Patrick Flynn would name Thomas Flynn as his father and supply the mother's currently unknown name — direct evidence of parentage from a primary-quality church original. Statewide civil marriage registration did not begin until 1913, so church records are the primary pre-1913 marriage source.

Search for the marriage record of Thomas Flynn and his wife — the candidate parents' marriage to each other. This is a mandatory FAN item for any parentage question: the couple's marriage record supplies the mother's maiden name (absent from every source found so far) and, by placing Thomas Flynn as a married man in Schuylkill County before 1845, corroborates his role as Patrick's father. Search Pennsylvania Church and Town Records 1708-1985 (collection 1401638) and Pennsylvania Compiled Marriages 1700-1900 (collection 1502909). Note: Thomas and his wife may have married in Ireland before emigrating — if no PA marriage is found, that negative result itself narrows the immigration window and supports an Ireland-marriage hypothesis requiring separate Ireland research.

Search Catholic parish baptismal registers for Schuylkill County in Pennsylvania Church and Town Records 1708-1985 (FamilySearch collection 1401638). Baptismal entries name both parents for each child — a baptism of any Flynn sibling in a Pottsville, Minersville, or Tamaqua Catholic parish (St. Patrick's Pottsville, St. Michael's Minersville, Annunciation Shenandoah) would name Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents. If Patrick himself was baptized in Pennsylvania after arriving from Ireland as a young child, that record would be direct evidence of parentage. This is the richest FAN source for an Irish Catholic immigrant family: one register page can confirm both parents, their marriage, and the sibling set in a single original entry.

After identifying siblings from the 1850, 1860, and 1880 census household images (steps 1–2), search the Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906-1968 (FamilySearch collection 2255317) for each named sibling. A sibling's death certificate names the same parents as Patrick's — providing independent corroboration of Thomas Flynn as father from a separate informant and separate record. Under the GPS, multiple independent sources converging on the same conclusion strengthen the proof. A sibling-informant would also likely have better knowledge of the family's parentage than Patrick's son-in-law (the informant on Patrick's own death certificate), making the sibling's record a higher-quality corroborating source.

Search for Thomas Flynn's own death record. If he died after 1906, search Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906-1968 (FamilySearch collection 2255317); his death certificate would name a surviving child as informant — that child would have firsthand knowledge of Thomas's family, making their information primary quality. If Thomas died before 1906 (likely given no probate was found and Patrick co-resided in 1860), search church burial registers in Pennsylvania Church and Town Records (collection 1401638) for entries in Schuylkill County Catholic parishes. Thomas's death record may also list his wife, closing the gap on the unknown mother.

Search naturalization records for Thomas Flynn as a fallback if no marriage record is located in step 4. Pre-1906 naturalizations occurred in any Pennsylvania court of record — Schuylkill County Court of Quarter Sessions or Common Pleas. Naturalization papers sometimes name a wife and children, or at minimum establish Thomas Flynn as an Irish immigrant resident in Schuylkill County during the relevant period, corroborating the hypothesis that he is the Thomas Flynn in the 1850 and 1860 census households. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania naturalization records; also check Ancestry for Schuylkill County court minutes.

---

## Research Plan — pl_003
**Question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?
**Purpose:** Fill the 1860–1908 census gap; look for Thomas Flynn co-residing with Patrick as an adult.

*(Note: the 1890 census is deliberately absent — it was almost entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire, with no surviving Schuylkill County schedules.)*

---

### Step 1 — pli_007 | 1870 Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill County
Patrick is ~25 and may have formed his own household; Thomas is ~52 and may be living with him or nearby. The last confirmed evidence is the 1860 co-residence, so this extends the chain into Patrick's adulthood. Search both names.

### Step 2 — pli_008 | 1880 Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill County ⭐ Highest priority
**The most evidentially important item in the plan.** The 1880 census is the first to state each person's explicit relationship to the household head. If Thomas (~62) is in Patrick's (~35) household, his entry would read "father" — that is *direct* parentage evidence, a qualitative leap beyond the *indirect* household-position inference the 1850 and 1860 censuses support. A positive 1880 result would materially advance the proof summary from *Probable* toward *Proved*.

### Step 3 — pli_009 | 1900 Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill County
Patrick is ~55, seven years before his death. Thomas would be ~82 if still living. The 1900 census also records birth month and year, which can refine Patrick's estimated 1845 birth date. Any Thomas co-residence continues to build the corroborating case.

---

### Step 4 — pli_010 | 1870 Census | Ancestry | Pennsylvania statewide *(fallback for Step 1)*
If FamilySearch finds no match, Ancestry's independent 1870 index may capture spelling variants (*Flin*, *Flinn*). Scope widened to all of Pennsylvania in case Patrick had moved to an adjacent county (Northumberland, Carbon, Dauphin) by age 25.

### Step 5 — pli_011 | 1880 Census | Ancestry | Pennsylvania statewide *(fallback for Step 2)*
Given how valuable a 1880 co-residence result would be, a second independent index is especially worth running. Ancestry's Soundex coverage is independent of FamilySearch's.

### Step 6 — pli_012 | 1900 Census | Ancestry | Pennsylvania statewide *(fallback for Step 3)*
If FamilySearch finds no 1900 match, broaden to statewide and also search Thomas Flynn independently — by 1900 he may have moved to a different child's household outside Schuylkill County.

---

Now I have everything I need. Let me build the plan in one batched `research_append` call.

**Survey findings:**
- **Death certificate** (1908): PA civil registration began 1906 — Patrick's cert should exist and will name parents directly
- **Census**: 1860 and 1880 are the key censuses; 1880 adds relationship column + parents' birthplace fields
- **Probate**: FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994" (indexed, collection 1417683) + browse-only image group 007936749 (1851–1930) as fallback
- **Thomas Flynn's marriage**: Required dedicated item per record-type-guide; church records (collection 1401638) + browse-only image group 007936751 as fallback
- **Naturalization**: Browse-only image group 007936750 (Schuylkill Co., 1849–1915)
- **Catholic sacramental records**: Diocese of Scranton Archives (not digitized online)
- No known record destruction issues for Schuylkill County

**Plan ID will be pl_002; plan items pli_004 through pli_012.**Plan written successfully — validated with no warnings. Here is the research plan:

---

## Research Plan: pl_002
**Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845 Ireland, d. 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, PA)?

---

### Step 1 — Patrick's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate *(pli_004)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Collection:** Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968 (collection 2255317, indexed, free)

Patrick died in 1908 — two years after Pennsylvania began mandatory civil death registration. Death certificates of this era include lines for the **father's name** and **mother's maiden name**. If completed by a knowledgeable informant, this is the single most direct evidence available for the parentage question and is the right first search.

---

### Step 2 — 1860 federal census, Schuylkill County *(pli_005)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch (indexed, free)

At ~15 years old in 1860, Patrick was likely still in Thomas Flynn's household. A second consecutive co-enumeration with Thomas would substantially strengthen the parent-child inference. This search also aims to capture **Thomas Flynn's wife's name**, which was not recorded in 1850, and any other children in the household.

---

### Step 3 — 1880 federal census, Schuylkill County *(pli_006)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch (indexed, free)

The 1880 census is the most informative pre-1900 enumeration: it adds a **relationship-to-head column** and dedicated fields for **each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace**. Patrick (~35) should be enumerable. Also searching for Thomas Flynn's 1880 presence or absence narrows the death window for the probate search in step 4.

---

### Step 4 — Thomas Flynn's probate records, Schuylkill County *(pli_007)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Collection:** Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 (collection 1417683, indexed)
**Date range targeted:** 1860–1900

Thomas (b. ~1818) likely died in this window. A will naming Patrick as a son or heir is **direct parentage evidence** — one of the strongest record types for this goal. An intestate administration filing lists heirs. Also searching for guardianship records involving Thomas's minor children.

---

### ↳ Step 5 — Schuylkill County probate, browse-only volumes *(pli_008)* — *fallback for step 4*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Image group:** 007936749 (1851–1930, 412 images, unindexed)

If the indexed collection in step 4 misses Thomas Flynn due to transcription errors on Irish surnames, browse the original probate volumes directly.

---

### Step 6 — Thomas Flynn and wife's marriage record *(pli_009)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Collection:** Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 (collection 1401638)
**Date range targeted:** 1838–1850

This is a **required dedicated item** for any parentage plan. A marriage record: (a) confirms Thomas and wife as a married couple; (b) supplies the **mother's maiden name**, which no census or death record in this period provides; and (c) a marriage date before Patrick's ~1845 birth corroborates Thomas as father. A negative result here likely means the marriage occurred in Ireland — not a disproof, just a shift to Irish records.

---

### ↳ Step 7 — Schuylkill County church records, browse-only volumes *(pli_010)* — *fallback for step 6*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Image group:** 007936751 (1842–1895, 187 images, unindexed, English and German)

If step 6 yields nothing, browse these original registers. Also look for **baptismal entries for siblings of Patrick born in Pennsylvania** — a sibling's baptism naming Thomas and wife as parents would supply the mother's name without requiring the marriage record itself.

---

### Step 8 — Schuylkill County naturalization records for Thomas Flynn *(pli_011)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · **Image group:** 007936750 (1849–1915, 298 images, unindexed) + indexed PA naturalization collections
**Date range targeted:** 1845–1880

Thomas (b. ~1818 Ireland) likely naturalized in the county courts (pre-1906 PA naturalizations were local). First papers may name birthplace and port of arrival. If Patrick also naturalized citing **derivative citizenship through his father**, that filing directly links the two men in a legal document.

---

### Step 9 — Diocese of Scranton Archives: Catholic sacramental records *(pli_012)*
**Repository:** Diocese of Scranton (dioceseofscranton.org) · **Also:** Historical Society of Schuylkill County (hschuylkill.org)
**Date range targeted:** 1845–1880

Schuylkill County's large Irish Catholic mining community maintained parish registers not fully digitized online. Baptismal records name the child, father, mother, and godparents. **A sibling's baptism naming Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents** would confirm the family unit and supply the mother's maiden name. Marriage registers may capture Thomas Flynn's own marriage if it occurred in a Pennsylvania parish.

---

### Plan summary

| # | Record type | Repository | Target date | Fallback? |
|---|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | Death certificate | FamilySearch (indexed) | 1908 | — |
| 2 | 1860 census | FamilySearch (indexed) | 1860 | — |
| 3 | 1880 census | FamilySearch (indexed) | 1880 | — |
| 4 | Probate (Thomas Flynn) | FamilySearch (indexed) | 1860–1900 | — |
| 5 | Probate browse-only | FamilySearch (image group) | 1851–1930 | ↳ fallback for #4 |
| 6 | Marriage (Thomas & wife) | FamilySearch church records | 1838–1850 | — |
| 7 | Church records browse-only | FamilySearch (image group) | 1842–1895 | ↳ fallback for #6 |
| 8 | Naturalization (Thomas Flynn) | FamilySearch / county courts | 1845–1880 | — |
| 9 | Catholic sacramental records | Diocese of Scranton / HSSC | 1845–1880 | — |

**9 searches total** (7 primary items + 2 fallbacks). Steps 1–3 are free and indexed on FamilySearch; steps 4–5 are free; step 9 requires contacting a physical archive.

---

**Recommended first search:** The 1908 **death certificate** (step 1) has the highest probability of naming both parents directly and should be searched first. Would you like me to execute the plan starting there?

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. Pennsylvania began mandatory civil death registration in 1906, so his death certificate should exist. Death certificates of this era include lines for the decedent's father's name and mother's maiden name — if completed by a knowledgeable informant, this is the most direct evidence available for Patrick's parents. Search FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968' (collection 2255317), which is fully indexed and freely accessible.

In 1860 Patrick Flynn would be approximately 15 years old — still likely living in Thomas Flynn's household. Finding Patrick enumerated with Thomas again in 1860 demonstrates continued co-residence across two census cycles, substantially strengthening the parent-child inference beyond the single 1850 co-enumeration. Also search separately for Thomas Flynn as head of household to identify his wife's name (not captured in 1850) and any other children enumerated. The 1860 census is fully indexed on FamilySearch.

The 1880 census is the most genealogically informative pre-1900 enumeration: it introduces a 'relationship to head of household' column and adds dedicated fields for each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace. Patrick Flynn (age ~35 in 1880) should appear in Schuylkill County. His stated parents' birthplaces will either corroborate Thomas Flynn's Irish origin or reveal a discrepancy requiring follow-up. Also search for Thomas Flynn in 1880: his presence or absence helps establish the window in which to search for probate records.

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818 Ireland) likely died between 1860 and 1900. Schuylkill County probate records are indexed on FamilySearch in 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683). A will naming Patrick Flynn as a son or heir is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — among the strongest record types for establishing parentage. An intestate administration will list heirs, which may include Patrick. Also search for guardianship filings, which could name minor Flynn children and their father.

Browse-only image group 007936749 covers Schuylkill County probate records 1851–1930 (412 images, 0% indexed). If the indexed FamilySearch collection in step 4 does not surface Thomas Flynn's estate — possible given transcription errors on Irish surnames — browse these original volumes directly for the likely death window identified in the census searches.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required by parentage research standards: (a) it confirms Thomas and wife as a married couple; (b) it supplies the mother's maiden name, which no census record in this period provides; and (c) a marriage date falling before Patrick's ~1845 birth corroborates Thomas as father. Thomas and wife likely married ca. 1838–1844 — either in Ireland before emigrating or in Pennsylvania shortly after arrival. Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638), covering Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, and other congregations. A negative result here does not disprove the marriage — it may simply mean they married in Ireland.

Browse-only image group 007936751 covers Schuylkill County church records 1842-1895 (187 images, 0% indexed, in English and German). If the indexed collection in step 6 does not yield a Thomas Flynn marriage, browse these original registers. Also look for baptismal entries for children of Thomas Flynn born in Pennsylvania — a sibling's baptism naming Thomas and wife as parents would directly supply the mother's full name and confirm the family unit independent of the census.

Before 1906, Pennsylvania naturalizations were filed in any court of record, including the county prothonotary and Quarter Sessions. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818 Ireland) likely naturalized between 1845 and 1870. Schuylkill County naturalization records 1849–1915 survive as browse-only image group 007936750 (298 images, 0% indexed). First papers (declarations of intent) often name the applicant's country of origin and sometimes birthplace or port of arrival. If Patrick Flynn also filed naturalization papers citing derivative citizenship through his father, this directly links the two men in a legal document. Also search FamilySearch's indexed Pennsylvania naturalization collections.

Schuylkill County's large Irish Catholic mining community kept sacramental registers at local parishes. These fall within the Diocese of Scranton (established 1868; previously Diocese of Philadelphia and Harrisburg). Baptismal registers name the child, father, mother, and godparents — an entry for a sibling of Patrick born in Pennsylvania would name Thomas Flynn as father and supply the mother's maiden name. Marriage registers would capture Thomas Flynn's own marriage if it occurred in a Pennsylvania parish. Contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives (dioceseofscranton.org) for access to pre-1900 sacramental records for Schuylkill County parishes. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County (hschuylkill.org) may also hold transcriptions or microfilm of local Catholic registers not available online.

Also search separately for Thomas Flynn as head of household to identify his wife's name (not captured in 1850) and any other children enumerated. The 1860 census is fully indexed on FamilySearch.

The 1880 census is the most genealogically informative pre-1900 enumeration: it introduces a 'relationship to head of household' column and adds dedicated fields for each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace. Patrick Flynn (age ~35 in 1880) should appear in Schuylkill County. His stated parents' birthplaces will either corroborate Thomas Flynn's Irish origin or reveal a discrepancy requiring follow-up. Also search for Thomas Flynn in 1880: his presence or absence helps establish the window in which to search for probate records.

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818 Ireland) likely died between 1860 and 1900. Schuylkill County probate records are indexed on FamilySearch in 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683). A will naming Patrick Flynn as a son or heir is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — among the strongest record types for establishing parentage. An intestate administration will list heirs, which may include Patrick. Also search for guardianship filings, which could name minor Flynn children and their father.

Browse-only image group 007936749 covers Schuylkill County probate records 1851–1930 (412 images, 0% indexed). If the indexed FamilySearch collection in step 4 does not surface Thomas Flynn's estate — possible given transcription errors on Irish surnames — browse these original volumes directly for the likely death window identified in the census searches.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required by parentage research standards: (a) it confirms Thomas and wife as a married couple; (b) it supplies the mother's maiden name, which no census record in this period provides; and (c) a marriage date falling before Patrick's ~1845 birth corroborates Thomas as father. Thomas and wife likely married ca. 1838–1844 — either in Ireland before emigrating or in Pennsylvania shortly after arrival. Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638), covering Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, and other congregations. A negative result here does not disprove the marriage — it may simply mean they married in Ireland.

Browse-only image group 007936751 covers Schuylkill County church records 1842-1895 (187 images, 0% indexed, in English and German). If the indexed collection in step 6 does not yield a Thomas Flynn marriage, browse these original registers. Also look for baptismal entries for children of Thomas Flynn born in Pennsylvania — a sibling's baptism naming Thomas and wife as parents would directly supply the mother's full name and confirm the family unit independent of the census.

Before 1906, Pennsylvania naturalizations were filed in any court of record, including the county prothonotary and Quarter Sessions. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818 Ireland) likely naturalized between 1845 and 1870. Schuylkill County naturalization records 1849–1915 survive as browse-only image group 007936750 (298 images, 0% indexed). First papers (declarations of intent) often name the applicant's country of origin and sometimes birthplace or port of arrival. If Patrick Flynn also filed naturalization papers citing derivative citizenship through his father, this directly links the two men in a legal document. Also search FamilySearch's indexed Pennsylvania naturalization collections.

Schuylkill County's large Irish Catholic mining community kept sacramental registers at local parishes. These fall within the Diocese of Scranton (established 1868; previously Diocese of Philadelphia and Harrisburg). Baptismal registers name the child, father, mother, and godparents — an entry for a sibling of Patrick born in Pennsylvania would name Thomas Flynn as father and supply the mother's maiden name. Marriage registers would capture Thomas Flynn's own marriage if it occurred in a Pennsylvania parish. Contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives (dioceseofscranton.org) for access to pre-1900 sacramental records for Schuylkill County parishes. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County (hschuylkill.org) may also hold transcriptions or microfilm of local Catholic registers not available online.

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — Parentage of Patrick Flynn (q_001)

**Goal:** Advance the parentage conclusion from *Probable* to *Proved* by filling the identified gaps: three unsearched census years, no identified mother, and no church or naturalization corroboration.

---

### Execution Order

| # | ID | Record type | Source | Target period | Notes |
|---|-----|-------------|--------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census | FamilySearch | 1880 | **Highest priority** — first census with relationship-to-head column; direct "son" entry possible |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census | FamilySearch | 1870 | Third decade of co-residence in Thomas's household would substantially strengthen parentage |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census | FamilySearch | 1900 | Last surviving census before Patrick's 1908 death; records parents' birthplaces |
| 4 | pli_010 | Census | Ancestry | 1880 | *Fallback for pli_007* — independent indexing if FamilySearch misses the entry |
| 5 | pli_011 | Census (FAN) | FamilySearch | 1870 | Search for **Thomas Flynn** specifically — names his wife and identifies Patrick's siblings |
| 6 | pli_012 | Marriage record | FamilySearch (collection 1921317) | 1885–1910 | Patrick's own marriage — may name parents; identifies wife for FAN research |
| 7 | pli_013 | Marriage record (browse) | FamilySearch vol. 007936753 | 1870–1910 | *Fallback for pli_012* — 0% indexed; browse by year if Patrick's marriage predates 1885 |
| 8 | pli_014 | Church records | FamilySearch (collection 1401638) | 1840–1880 | **Dedicated search for Thomas Flynn's marriage to his wife** — the only way to identify Patrick's mother |
| 9 | pli_015 | Church records (browse) | FamilySearch vol. 007936751 | 1842–1895 | *Fallback for pli_014* — 0% indexed; Catholic/Protestant registers including English and German |
| 10 | pli_016 | Naturalization | FamilySearch vol. 007936750 | 1849–1915 | Thomas Flynn's declaration/petition — names wife and children; confirms Irish origin; 0% indexed |

---

### Key Rationale

- **pli_007 (1880 census)** is the single most important unsearched record. It is the first census that directly states household relationships — if Patrick is listed as Thomas's son, that is direct evidence of parentage, the strongest census proof possible.
- **pli_014 (Thomas Flynn's marriage)** addresses the most significant current gap: Patrick's mother is completely unknown. No record found so far names her. Thomas and wife's marriage record would supply her maiden name.
- **pli_011 (Thomas Flynn FAN)** is the collateral search: even without Patrick present, Thomas's 1870 household identifies siblings and names the wife, enabling FAN pivot if Patrick proves elusive.
- Fallback chains: pli_010 ← pli_007; pli_013 ← pli_012; pli_015 ← pli_014.

---

**10 searches total.** Would you like to start executing — beginning with the 1880 census (pli_007)?

args.query: Irish immigrant naturalization records Pennsylvania county court 1850 1870

entry.rationale: The 1880 federal census is the first to record the relationship of each person to the head of household. If Patrick Flynn (age ~35) is still in Thomas Flynn's household, his entry would directly state 'son' — the strongest census evidence available for this parentage question. Even if Patrick has established his own household by 1880, the 1880 census records his parents' birthplaces (a new column), corroborating Ireland as his origin and Thomas's identity. Thomas (age ~62) would also likely still be enumerated; his household would show remaining children and confirm family continuity. This is the highest-priority unsearched record.

In 1870, Patrick would be approximately 25 and Thomas approximately 52 — Thomas was very likely still living and present in Schuylkill County. Finding Patrick in Thomas's household for a third consecutive decade (1850, 1860, 1870) would substantially strengthen the parentage inference through persistent co-residence evidence. Even if Patrick has established his own household by 1870, his presence in the same county and Thomas's continued presence corroborate the relationship. The 1870 census does not state relationships, so this provides indirect evidence, but a third consecutive census placement carries significant cumulative weight.

The 1900 census is the last surviving federal census before Patrick's death in March 1908 (the 1890 census is largely destroyed nationwide). Patrick (age ~55) would be alive and enumerated. The 1900 census records parents' birthplaces for each individual, which would corroborate Ireland as his origin and could name other family members in his household — wife, children, or relatives — opening additional FAN research avenues. Any siblings of Patrick who appear in the same neighborhood also constitute indirect evidence of Thomas Flynn's parentage.

Independent Ancestry indexing of the 1880 census as a cross-check if the FamilySearch search (pli_007) does not locate Patrick Flynn. Ancestry and FamilySearch use different transcribers; surname spelling variations (Flynn / Flinn / Flin) or enumerator handwriting may be interpreted differently between indexes. Execute only if pli_007 is inconclusive or Patrick is not found.

FAN item: search specifically for Thomas Flynn (head of household, age ~52 in 1870) regardless of whether Patrick is found in pli_008. Thomas's 1870 household entry independently establishes his continued presence in Schuylkill County, names his wife (whose name no record found so far has captured), and identifies other children who may be researched as collateral relatives. If Thomas has other adult children still at home, their co-residence strengthens the inference that Thomas is the patriarch of a multi-child Irish household — consistent with Patrick's parentage.

Patrick Flynn had at least one daughter who had married before 1908 (the death certificate informant was son-in-law James Brown). Patrick likely married circa 1865–1885. Search the FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950' (collection 1921317) for Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County. His marriage record could name his parents directly, identify his wife (enabling FAN research into her family's knowledge of the Flynns), and establish the family's religious affiliation — pointing to the correct church register for subsequent searches.

Browse-only fallback for pli_012. FamilySearch holds a Schuylkill County marriage records image group (007936753, 1870–1910) that is 0% indexed. If Patrick's marriage predates the 1885 coverage of the indexed collection, or if his name does not appear in the index, this volume must be browsed manually by year. Execute only if pli_012 does not locate the marriage.

Dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn to his wife — the candidate parents' marriage to each other. No record found so far identifies Patrick's mother; this is the primary means of doing so. Pennsylvania civil registration of marriages did not begin until 1913, so church registers are the source. Search FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (collection 1401638) for Thomas Flynn's marriage in Schuylkill County. Thomas married before Patrick's birth (~1845); if the marriage occurred in Pennsylvania after immigration (ca. 1840s), Catholic or Protestant registers in the county would record it. Identifying the wife's maiden name is essential to completing the parentage picture — Thomas is named by three lines of evidence, but the mother is entirely unknown.

The 1787 Danish national census (col. 1401625) lists household members with ages and relationships. Danish military levying rolls (lægdsruller, col. 2513529) enrolled males from early childhood; Mikkel (b. 1779) would appear from approximately the 1789 session onward. The 1801 Danish census (col. 1401625) shows Mikkel at approximately age 22. If he remained in the parental household, the father Niels should appear; if Mikkel is listed as head of his own household, the father is likely deceased by then. Corroborates or extends evidence from the 1787 census entry. Also available at Dansk Demografisk Database.

When father Niels died, a skifte (probate) proceeding would record his name, widow, and all heirs — including Mikkel. Probate also corroborates the father's full name if other records name him only by patronymic. Originals are browse-only images after the index.

### Research Plan — q_001
*When and where did Elijah Wilkins (b. c. 1814, Muhlenberg Co., KY) die?*

> ⚠️ **Coverage caveat:** Statewide Kentucky *death certificates* began **1 January 1911** only. The question's "certificates" in practice means the county-level death registers kept 1852–1859 and 1874–1910 — the pre-1911 equivalent. If Elijah died before 1874, even those registers likely won't cover him, and probate + mortality schedule become primary.

| # | Source | Repository | Period | Why |
|---|--------|------------|--------|-----|

| **pli_002** | Muhlenberg Co. death register images (vol. 007991103) — browse | FamilySearch | 1852–1910 | Only 4% indexed; browsing images captures what pli_001 misses; null result guides next steps |
| **pli_003** *(fallback for pli_001)* | *Kentucky, U.S., Death Records, 1852–1965* | Ancestry | 1852–1965 | Separate transcription project; covers some entries not in FamilySearch |
| **pli_004** | 1880 Federal Mortality Schedule | FamilySearch | 1880 | Direct evidence if death fell mid-1879 to June 1880; also serves as a bracket when absent |
| **pli_005** | *Kentucky Probate Records, 1727–1990* — Muhlenberg Co. vol. 007834412 | FamilySearch | 1858–1889 | Primary death evidence when registers have gaps; administrator bond dates bracket death closely |
| **pli_006** | Find A Grave — Muhlenberg Co. cemeteries | Find A Grave | 1860–1900 | Headstones record death dates independently of civil registration; FAN cluster graves confirm identity |

**Scope:** 6 searches across FamilySearch and Find A Grave (Ancestry only if pli_001 is negative).

args.query: Kentucky death certificates vital records genealogy

entry.rationale: Kentucky statewide death certificates did not begin until 1 January 1911 — the question's reference to 'certificates' in practice means the county-level death registers kept 1852-1859 and 1874-1910, which are their pre-1911 equivalent. The FamilySearch index 'Kentucky, Deaths and Burials, 1843-1970' (collection 1804888) is the fastest entry point: a hit would directly supply the date and place of death and confirm identity. Coverage is partial for the 1860s-1870s, but if Elijah survived past 1874 — which is plausible for a man born c. 1814 — the register may capture him.

The Muhlenberg County death register image group (007991103, 1852-1910) is only 4% indexed, meaning roughly 96% of entries are invisible to the collection-1804888 search. Browsing the images directly is essential to catch entries the index misses, especially for the 1870s where indexing is sparsest. Even a null result here is informative: if Elijah's name is absent from the surviving pages, it implies either death before 1874 or a registration gap — directing attention to probate and mortality schedules.

Ancestry's 'Kentucky, U.S., Death Records, 1852-1965' (collection 1222) indexes some county-register entries that FamilySearch lacks — the two indexes were compiled from overlapping but distinct transcription projects. If item 1 yields nothing, Ancestry may carry the entry. Searches item 1's index before paying the subscription cost of this source.

The 1880 Federal Mortality Schedule captures every person who died in the 12 months preceding the June 1880 census enumeration. If Elijah died mid-1879 through June 1880, the schedule is direct evidence of his death date, cause, and county. It also provides a bracket: absence from the 1880 mortality schedule and from the 1880 population census together narrow the death window to before mid-1879 or after June 1880.

For Kentucky deaths not captured by the gappy county death registers, probate is the primary death evidence. When a person died, the county court required an administrator bond (naming the deceased and the date the estate was opened) and later estate settlements — together they bracket the death date closely. FamilySearch 'Kentucky Probate Records, 1727-1990' (collection 1875188) holds browse-only Muhlenberg County volumes; image group 007834412 (1858-1889) covers the full likely death window. A will or administrator bond naming Elijah's heirs would also confirm identity and supply family context.

Find A Grave's Muhlenberg County cemetery index captures headstone inscriptions and burial transcriptions that record death dates independently of civil registration. Cemetery evidence is especially valuable here because the county death registers have known gaps for the 1860s-1870s. A headstone or transcription for Elijah Wilkins would supply a death date and burial place, and neighboring graves of family members (FAN cluster) may corroborate identity.

Here's a full review of the active research plan for the parentage question.

---

## Research Plan Review — q_001: *Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?*

**Active plan:** `pl_002` (created 2026-05-02)

### What's Been Done

| # | Item | Record Type | Status | What It Found |
|---|------|-------------|--------|---------------|
| pli_004 | 1860 Census, Schuylkill Co. — FamilySearch | census | ✅ completed | Patrick Flynn, age 15, in Thomas Flynn household (dwelling 112) — indirect evidence of parentage |
| pli_005 | Death Certificate, 1908 — FamilySearch | vital_record | ✅ completed | Names Thomas Flynn as father; informant was son-in-law James Brown (secondary for parentage) |
| pli_006 | Probate, Schuylkill Co., 1870–1890 — FamilySearch | probate | 🔄 **in_progress** | ← **Next step** |

---

### Next Step: Complete pli_006 — Thomas Flynn Probate Records

The probate search is already underway. There's a notable detail worth flagging: **`src_006` already exists in the sources array** — a FamilySearch record for the *Schuylkill County will of Thomas Flynn, 1881* (ARK: `ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS4V-7TXW`). However:

- `pli_006` is still marked `in_progress` (not completed)
- `src_006` has no associated log entry (`log_entry_id: null`)
- **No assertions have been extracted from the will** — nothing from src_006 appears in `assertions[]`

This means the will was located but not yet fully processed. The immediate next action is:

> **→ Run `record-extraction` on the Thomas Flynn will (src_006)** to extract its assertions (e.g., does the will explicitly name Patrick as a son?), then mark `pli_006` as completed.

Once that's done, if the will names Patrick, hypothesis `h_001` advances from *supported* to *strongly supported*, and the proof summary (currently rated **Probable**) may be upgradeable to **Proved** — which is exactly what ps_001 flags as the tipping condition.

---

### A Note on Plan Breadth

The active plan (3 items) focused on census, vital records, and probate. Once pli_006 is wrapped up, the plan will be exhausted — but q_001 will likely still warrant follow-up on:

- **1870, 1880, 1900 censuses** (explicitly flagged as gaps in ps_001)
- **Thomas Flynn's marriage** to establish the mother's identity (not yet addressed at all)
- Other sources already extracted but not yet assertion-linked (src_005 baptism, src_007–src_009)

Those would call for a new plan (`pl_003`) after pl_002 is marked completed, which we can kick off after the will extraction is done.

**Ready to proceed?** Say "extract the will" and I'll run record-extraction on `src_006`, or "start searching" to continue with pli_006's probate search on FamilySearch.

## Research Plan pl_002 — q_001: Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?

**Context:** Patrick Flynn, born ca. 1845 in Ireland, died 1908 in Schuylkill County, PA. The 1850 census places him (age 5) in the household of Thomas Flynn (head, ~born 1818, Ireland). That co-enumeration is indirect evidence only — no source yet states the parentage explicitly, and Patrick's mother is completely unidentified. Hypothesis h_001 (Thomas Flynn = father) awaits corroboration.

---

### Step 1 — Patrick Flynn's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate · `pli_004` · FamilySearch
Pennsylvania civil death registration began 1906; Patrick's 1908 death must have generated a certificate. Death certificates of this era name the father and the mother's maiden name — **the single highest-probability source for direct parentage evidence**. The informant's identity (a knowledgeable child or spouse vs. a distant acquaintance) will govern how much weight the parentage entries carry.

### Step 2 — Same death certificate · `pli_005` · Ancestry *(fallback for step 1)*
Ancestry holds an independent index and image set for Pennsylvania death certificates, 1906–1963. Search here if FamilySearch returns no match or the image is illegible.

### Step 3 — 1860 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County · `pli_006` · FamilySearch
Patrick (~age 15) was almost certainly still in Thomas Flynn's household. A second co-enumeration 10 years after 1850 significantly strengthens h_001. Thomas's 1860 household will also show his **wife's name** — the only parent not yet identified — and any siblings for cross-referencing later.

### Step 4 — 1870 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County · `pli_007` · FamilySearch
Patrick (~25) may have formed his own household by 1870, but Thomas's household — if he was still living — could show his wife by name. Bridges the 1860–1880 gap; the 1890 census is largely destroyed.

### Step 5 — 1880 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County · `pli_008` · FamilySearch
The first census with an **explicit relationship-to-head column**. If Patrick is still with Thomas, the stated relationship (son, boarder, etc.) is direct census evidence — a qualitative improvement over 1850. Even in his own household, Patrick's entry will list father's and mother's birthplaces, testing the Irish-origin claim.

### Step 6 — 1900 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County · `pli_009` · FamilySearch
The 1900 census records **father's birthplace, mother's birthplace, and years in the U.S.** for every person. Patrick (~55, died 1908) would appear here; these columns can confirm both parents were born in Ireland and supply an immigration-year estimate that narrows the passenger-list search.

### Step 7 — Thomas Flynn probate / will, Schuylkill County · `pli_010` · FamilySearch
FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994" (2.3 million images) covers Schuylkill County Orphans' Court. If Thomas left a will naming Patrick as son or heir, that is **direct parentage evidence** — the strongest corroboration of h_001 a single document could provide. Estate papers also typically name the widow (Patrick's mother) and all heirs.

### Step 8 — Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother (church records) · `pli_011` · FamilySearch
*Dedicated parentage-plan item required by BCG Standard 14.* A marriage record establishes the **mother's maiden name** — information census records and most death certificates don't carry — and by its date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 birth, confirms the couple were together before he was born. PA civil marriage registration didn't begin until 1913; the pre-1906 substitute is church registers. FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985" covers Catholic, Lutheran, and Reformed congregations; St. Patrick's Pottsville, St. Clair, and Minersville are the most likely Catholic repositories for the Schuylkill coal region.

### Step 9 — Catholic parish records, Schuylkill County, 1845–1880 · `pli_012` · FamilySearch
A broader search for baptisms and confirmations of Patrick's younger siblings born in Pennsylvania. Each sibling baptism names both parents, and could supply the mother's given name that even the marriage record may not preserve in indexed form. Diocese of Harrisburg (formed 1868 from Philadelphia) covers Schuylkill County.

### Step 10 — Passenger lists, Flynn family from Ireland, 1845–1850 · `pli_013` · FamilySearch
Patrick was born ca. 1845 in Ireland; the family arrived before the 1850 census, likely during the Great Famine (1845–1852). Philadelphia and New York manifests from this window survive on FamilySearch and may name Thomas Flynn's wife and traveling children — independent evidence of household composition **before** the 1850 census. Lower probability given sparse pre-1895 manifests, but the only identified remaining path to the mother's name if church and probate records yield nothing.

---

### Fallback relationship
Step 2 (Ancestry death cert) is the fallback for Step 1 (FamilySearch death cert). All other steps are independent searches.

10 searches across FamilySearch (9) and Ancestry (1).

---

## Plan pl_002 — Active

**Question:** q_001 (parentage, in progress)
**Current status:** Two of three items completed; one in progress.

---

### Step 1 — 1860 U.S. Census · FamilySearch ✅ Completed
**Jurisdiction:** Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania | **Date range:** 1860
**Rationale:** Confirm Patrick still in Thomas Flynn household in 1860. Strengthens parent-child identification.
**Outcome (log_004):** Patrick Flynn, age 15, found in Thomas Flynn's household — consistent with the 1850 placement.

---

### Step 2 — Death Certificate (Vital Record) · FamilySearch ✅ Completed
**Jurisdiction:** Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania | **Date range:** 1908
**Rationale:** Death certificate may name parents directly.
**Outcome (log_005):** Death certificate (no. 4521) names Thomas Flynn as father. Informant was son-in-law James Brown — secondary for parentage facts.

---

### Step 3 — Probate Records · FamilySearch 🔄 In Progress
**Jurisdiction:** Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania | **Date range:** 1870–1890
**Rationale:** Thomas Flynn's probate/will may name Patrick as a son — direct evidence of parentage.
**Status:** Search underway; no result logged yet.

---

## What's Next

**Step 3 (probate)** is the active item. The search targets Thomas Flynn's will or estate in Schuylkill County on FamilySearch. If it returns Patrick by name as a son, that would be direct evidence upgrading the current "Probable" proof summary toward "Proved."

---

## A Note on Plan Scope

The proof summary (ps_001) itself flags several gaps not yet covered by this plan:
- **1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses** — not yet searched (the timeline shows a high-severity gap from 1860 to 1908)
- **Thomas Flynn's marriage record** — a dedicated parentage-plan item for the candidate parents' marriage (which would supply the mother's maiden name and corroborate the father identification) has not been planned

output.text_response:
Good — I can see the full project state. Here's what's happening:

- **Active plan `pl_002`** for q_001 has one unfinished item: `pli_006` (probate, **1870-1890**, `in_progress`)
- A new reference puts Thomas Flynn's death **~1865**, invalidating that date window
- This is **supersede mode**: `pl_002` has an unfinished item, but new information invalidates its premise — I'll supersede it and build a corrected `pl_003`

Let me survey what collections actually cover Schuylkill County in the 1860–1870 window before writing the new plan.Let me also check for mortality schedules and tax records specifically — key sources for pinning a death in the 1860s.Good data. Now I have what I need:

- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994** (FS collection 1417683) — covers the 1860-1870 window
- **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985** (FS collection 1401638) — church burials, Schuylkill County
- PA had **no statewide death registration until 1906** — so for a ~1865 death the key sources are: probate, mortality schedules (embedded in the 1860 and 1870 censuses), church burial registers, tax lists, and newspapers
- External links for 1860-1870 Schuylkill County are thin — Ancestry's PA tax records only reach to 1801; newspaper databases (Newspapers.com) are the main external avenue

**Planning mode confirmed: Supersede.** `pl_002` has an `in_progress` item (`pli_006`, probate 1870–1890) whose entire date premise is invalidated. I'll supersede it and build `pl_003` targeting 1860–1870 with the right record mix. Existing completed items (`pli_004`, `pli_005`) are unaffected — they stay in the audit trail on `pl_002`.

**New plan items** (pli_007–pli_013, superseding pli_006):All written cleanly — `pl_002` superseded, `pl_003` active with 7 items. Here's the revised plan:

---

## Revised Research Plan — `pl_003`
**Question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?
**Supersedes:** `pl_002` (date window 1870–1890 invalidated by new reference placing Thomas Flynn's death ~1865)

---

### Step 1 — Probate Records, 1860–1870 · FamilySearch · `pli_007` ⭐ Start here
*Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 (collection 1417683).* The primary correction: the prior plan searched 1870–1890; this targets the window consistent with a ~1865 death. A will or letters of administration would directly name heirs and anchor the death date.

### Step 2 — Probate Records, 1860–1870 · Ancestry · `pli_008` *(fallback for Step 1)*
Ancestry's independent probate index may surface records not yet available on FamilySearch, or transcribe the surname differently. Search if Step 1 yields nothing.

### Step 3 — 1860 Mortality Schedule · FamilySearch · `pli_009`
The mortality schedule lists everyone who died in Schuylkill County **June 1859–May 1860**. If Thomas died in that window, he appears here with age and cause of death. If he's absent, death is narrowed to post-June 1860.

### Step 4 — 1870 Census + Mortality Schedule · FamilySearch · `pli_010`
Two complementary searches: **(a)** negative search for Thomas — absence before June 1870 confirms a pre-1870 death consistent with ~1865; **(b)** search for his widow and surviving children (including Patrick) whose household may corroborate the parent-child relationship. Also check the **1870 mortality schedule** (deaths June 1869–May 1870) while here.

### Step 5 — Church Burial Records, 1860–1870 · FamilySearch · `pli_011`
St. Mary's Catholic, Pottsville, is already in this project (Patrick's 1845 baptism, src_005). If Thomas died Catholic, the burial register gives the exact death date and interment. *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (collection 1401638) covers Schuylkill County parishes.

### Step 6 — Tax Assessment Lists, 1860–1870 · FamilySearch · `pli_012`
Pennsylvania counties compiled annual tax lists. Thomas's last year of appearance + first year of absence (or widow as head of household) brackets the death year independently of all other sources.

### Step 7 — Newspaper Death Notices, 1860–1870 · Newspapers.com / GenealogyBank · `pli_013`
The *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville) regularly printed death notices. A notice for Thomas Flynn circa 1865 would confirm the death date and may name survivors — including Patrick. Search via Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank.

---

**Fallback relationship:** Step 2 (Ancestry probate) backs up Step 1 (FamilySearch probate).

**Estimated scope:** 7 searches across 3 repositories (FamilySearch, Ancestry, newspaper databases).

**Next step:** Ready to execute — shall I start with Step 1, the FamilySearch probate search for Thomas Flynn in the 1860–1870 window?

args.query: Pennsylvania mortality schedules 1860 1870 census death records Schuylkill County

entry.rationale: Primary target, corrected date window. A new reference places Thomas Flynn's death around 1865, not 1875–1890 as previously assumed. Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994 (collection 1417683) covers Schuylkill County probate through this period. A will or letters of administration dated 1860–1870 would directly name heirs — potentially confirming Patrick as a son — and would supply an approximate death date.

Fallback if FamilySearch (pli_007) yields nothing. Ancestry holds Pennsylvania probate indexes independently of FamilySearch and may index records not yet digitized on FamilySearch, or transcribe names differently.

The 1860 census mortality schedule lists every person who died in Schuylkill County in the 12 months preceding June 1, 1860 (i.e., June 1859–May 1860). If Thomas Flynn died within that window, he would appear here with approximate age and cause of death — anchoring the death year and supporting the ~1865 estimate if he does not appear (narrowing it to post-June 1860).

Two complementary searches on the 1870 census: (1) Negative search for Thomas Flynn — his absence confirms he died before June 1870, consistent with the ~1865 estimate. (2) Search for his widow and surviving children, including Patrick — if the family remains in Schuylkill County, the widow's household may identify other children and corroborate the parent-child relationship. The 1870 mortality schedule (deaths June 1869–May 1870) should also be checked: if Thomas died in that narrow window, he would appear there.

St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville, is already documented in this project (src_005 — Patrick Flynn's 1845 baptism). If Thomas Flynn died as a Catholic, the burial register would supply the death date, age, and interment location. Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985 (collection 1401638) covers Schuylkill County parishes. Search St. Mary's burial registers and other Pottsville-area Catholic parish registers for Thomas Flynn, 1860–1870.

Pennsylvania county tax assessment lists were compiled annually. Thomas Flynn's last year of appearance on the Schuylkill County tax list establishes a terminus post quem for his death; his first year of absence (or his widow's first year as head of household) establishes a terminus ante quem. Together these narrow the death year independently of the probate and census searches.

The Miners' Journal (Pottsville) was the principal newspaper of Schuylkill County and regularly published death notices and obituaries. A notice for Thomas Flynn circa 1865 would confirm the death date and may name survivors including Patrick. Search via Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank, both of which hold digitized runs of 19th-century Pennsylvania papers.
